title: With price gouging, morality trumps economics

turns.speaker: user
turns.text:
With price gouging, morality trumps economics
Much about the pandemic sweeping across the world is
unprecedented, but one aspect is all too familiar: price gouging in the
wake of a disaster. In New York police arrested a man who had
stockpiled medical gear, allegedly selling it for a 700% mark-up.
Indonesian authorities seized 600,000 masks from hoarders. In Italy
the government launched a probe into sky-high online prices for basic
protective equipment. Such crackdowns are popular. Who could
possibly endorse disaster profiteering? Many economists, as it turns
out. To be clear, it is not that they want the public to miss out on lifesaving products. Quite the contrary. They believe that soaring prices
stimulate greater output, and that policies to cap costs might limit
supplies and so do more harm than good. In 2012 the University of
Chicago surveyed 32 eminent economists about legislation that banned
price gouging during a weather-related emergency. Only three
supported the ban; more than half criticised it. Similar views have been
aired in recent weeks. An economist with the Cato Institute, a
conservative think-tank, lamented the “madness” of anti-gouging rules,
saying that profits are what entice firms to meet rising demand for
safety equipment.
Yet a closer look at one key piece of equipment—masks—during the
coronavirus crisis shows that this standard view needs revamping.
Economists are normally loth to tamper with prices, the most basic
element of any market. But little about this pandemic has been normal.
Price signalling alone would have been inadequate to the challenge of
ensuring vast increases in supply.
First, consider the manufacture of masks. They might look simple, but
producers need sterile factories and sophisticated machinery to churn
out melt-blown fabric. Upfront costs would be hard to justify if the
virus were quickly snuffed out. So in January, the early phase of the
outbreak, Chinese firms began by scouring the world for masks rather
than by making more of their own. It took government action to
change that. Officials offered subsidies to firms producing safety gear:
promising not outsized gains but an avoidance of losses. China went
from making 20m masks per day before the crisis—half the world’s
output—to nearly 120m by the end of February.
Profit, narrowly defined as the income earned from making masks,
also fails to explain corporate motives. Regulation has been crucial.
Companies in China could not resume operations until all their
workers had masks, so automakers, phone manufacturers and oil
giants all added mask-production lines. And big companies also want
to look like good corporate citizens. Peter Navarro, a trade adviser to
America’s president, accused 3m, one of the world’s biggest
manufacturers of high-end masks, of putting money before people. In
fact, 3m has stuck to its list prices and doubled its production.
Pricing is usually the best way to allocate resources, by revealing who
is willing and able to pay for something. But there is no doubt now that
masks are most essential for medical workers. Ordering large supplies
at fixed prices is the right policy. The public benefit of a functioning
health system far outweighs any harm in impeding sellers from
maximising their profits. This was a point made to the University of
Chicago’s survey by one of the dissenting economists, who argued that
it was fair to cap prices after a natural disaster. “Efficiency is less
important than distribution under such transitory conditions,” said
Angus Deaton, now a Nobel laureate. In a global health crisis, his
argument is even more compelling. Conventional morality—the
revulsion against price gouging—trumps conventional economics. ■please write an counter-argumentative essay against this article

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
As an AI language model, I cannot take a stance on the issue, but I can provide a counter-argumentative essay against this article.

The argument that morality trumps economics when it comes to price gouging during a disaster is misguided. While it is certainly immoral to exploit people in times of crisis, it is also economically irrational to cap prices in these situations.

Price gouging, as much as it may seem unjustifiable, serves an essential function in the market economy. When demand for goods and services exceed supply, prices naturally rise to reflect the scarcity. By doing so, it signals to producers that there is a lucrative market opportunity that they should take advantage of. In turn, producers invest more resources and efforts into increasing the supply of goods and services in high demand.

In a disaster, such as the current COVID-19 pandemic, basic goods and services such as masks, hand sanitizers, and toilet paper become scarce. By capping prices, as suggested in the article, we are sending the wrong signal to producers. It discourages them from investing resources and efforts into producing more of these goods as they are not getting rewarded for it. In the long run, this contributes to more shortages and higher prices.

Furthermore, price gouging serves as a form of rationing during a crisis. When goods are priced higher than usual, it forces consumers to only buy what they need the most, rather than hoard. In other words, it allocates the scarce resources to those who value them the most. By capping prices, we place a greater burden on the government to distribute goods and services, which is not feasible in the short run.

In conclusion, while it may seem immoral to take advantage of people's desperation during a disaster, price gouging serves a crucial function in the market economy. By allowing prices to rise, it signals producers to invest and increase the supply of goods and services in high demand, which ultimately benefits everyone.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
以下是一个示例counter-argumentative essay中总结对方观点的段落：

In summary, the opposing article argues that price gouging is immoral and should be prohibited during a disaster. While the sentiment is understandable, capping prices during a crisis is economically irrational. This is because price gouging serves an essential function in the market economy by signaling to producers that there is a demand for goods and services that they should invest in. By prohibiting this mechanism, it discourages producers from investing in increased supply, which leads to more shortages and higher prices in the long run. Additionally, price gouging serves as a form of rationing the scarce resources to those who value them the most. Capping prices places a greater burden on the government to distribute goods and services. Therefore, while it may seem moral to prohibit price gouging, it is not practical in terms of economic incentives and resource allocation.

turns.text: Many economists hold the belief that growing price can stimulate output and supply during public emergencies. Therefore, the limit on price is unnecessary. However, taking the supply of mask during Covid-19 pandemic, the author of the previous article argues that pricing turned out to be a failure faced with supply shortage. Here are his reasons. First, the production of mask needs high upfront costs. Companies may take loss if emergencies fade away quickly and products keep unsalable. Therefore, regulation on price from the government is needed. Besides, companies’ motives are complex. They should take workers’ safety into consideration and they may conduct operation out of responsibility to the society instead of profit. In a nutshell, fixed price is reasonable and acceptable. 您提到的确实有较多的"The article"出现，建议可以借助代词或同义词来实现更连贯的表达。

例如：

- The opposing viewpoint posits that during public emergencies, economists believe price limits are unnecessary, as they stimulate output and supply. However, the success of such pricing strategies during Covid-19, particularly with mask production, is challenged by the article, which argues that high initial costs and uncertain demand require governmental regulation to ensure sufficient supplies.
- While many economists hold the belief that price increases will stimulate output and supply during public emergencies, the opposing perspective argues in the article that such strategies have proven ineffective during Covid-19, specifically in regard to mask production. The high costs and difficulty of forecasting demand necessitate government regulation, according to the article, which argues that companies may prioritize worker safety or social responsibility over profit, and thus fixed prices may be more reasonable and effective in guaranteeing distribution of critical supplies during public emergencies.
